speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the family, I don't see die. But he's the one I saw, and this was the one that sticks with me. You know what I'm saying? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So what don't you see doesn't hurt, but this I saw with my own eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the way he was-- you ever saw when you pick up a fish when you go fishing? I saw where the poor guy was jumping left and right. He was a very powerful man. He was a champion boxer, a weightlifter. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's OK. I've been worse place.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about how you crossed the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I swim across the river. You know, the water was ice cold. Imagine, blocks of ice was thrown over there. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before I left the river, I took my jacket, and I dumped it inside the water, make sure I have some reserve water to drink. I don't know when I'm going to see another river again.

speaker: interviewer
text: From there, we were marching day in and day out. So one time at night, they stop us. And I hear, like that was a lot of forestry-- trees. So the people was so tired that they used to roll down from the tree, you know? They could not figure out, and they'd go down, their bodies, to get killed or hurt very bad. So what I did, I took my jacket, I put it on my waist, and I tied it with my tree. In case I move around, I won't go down. That's the way I passed the night at the place. So in the morning, we have to keep walk. You know what I mean? I tied myself with a tree. That way, I won't slide down the thing over there. So some people didn't. There was a big hill. They go down. They used to get all crushed with another tree or get killed as they went down. They used to roll down, because it was very much like a slope. Because from the other side, they don't want the Germans to see what's going on, you know? Because there was houses from the other side. So that's where you slept-- on the slope?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the slope, yes. But I took my jacket-- the wet jacket I had. I put it around here, and the arms, I tied with a tree. That  way, I won't roll over. So that way, I got up in the morning. I was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to walk from [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] sunup to sundown. We used to keep walking. No food, no nothing-- the food they gave us in Warsaw, that was the end of the food. So where you stop at night, I used to have a spoon with me. And I used to dig around and look what I can find under the ground. So at night, I feel. If it's something soft, it's not a stone, so I used to eat it. It was any kind of roots. Because with hunger, everything tastes good. Because when you dig that thing, and you find a stone, if it's too hard, then it means a stone. I don't want to eat that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I dig it out, then find the different type of roots under the ground. And I keep eating the roots. It gets you moisture and everything. And that way, I was going maybe for quite a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after about 10 days walking, half of the people died already on the way, maybe more. There are trucks in the back, and they used to dump in the truck, and what happened, I don't know-- anybody who stood behind. You have to continue with the group. So when we went up on a hill somewhere again, to cross the hill, it was at night, and it was drizzling ice. It was so cold. So I was with another guy I knew. We were walking next to another. We used to walk five, five, five, five.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, a German guard comes to me, and he says, hey Juden. It means, 'Hey, Jew.' You take this bicycle to me. Take up to the hill. And on top, I'm going to wait for you. You give it to me. Because he wants me to carry the bike on the top. And in the back he had a backpack, in the back of the bike, tied up in the back seat in the bike.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do I do? I cannot say no. He's got the rifle and can shoot me on the spot if I say anything not right. So I took with the other. I said to the other guy, please help me, because I cannot do it by myself. So the guy, OK, I'm going to help you. He was up from the front, and I was pushing from the back, the bike up in the hill. All of a sudden, I put the hand in there, and I find something warm there. I was freezing to death. Something is nice and  warm here. What is going on here? So the other guy, he says to me, listen, it's too dark. He will never recognize. Let's see what's inside there. What the hell, this guy-- we are so many thousands of people here. We all look alike, you know? All dead, skin and bones, you know? We don't look-- we're all the same, no hair. So OK, OK, let's open up a little. But I was holding it, and he was opening it. We opened it. We find a bread inside, a big salami. But a little hot tea inside with sugar. My god, that's heaven on earth. So he had a lot of food there, they gave him for the 19 days' food. So we opened the rucksack. So he hold the rucksack. We went close to where we was crossing a bridge. He pushed the bike from the bridge over the water. The Germans arrived, who  opened a big light over there. They thought somebody probably jumped there, and they start shooting in the water there. But in the meantime, we had the backpack. And me and my friend, we start eating. We swallow everything like any-- my god, this is hot tea with sugar, hot. What is that? So we drink everything. We eat everything. So after an hour or two, the German guard was waiting on the top of the mountain, and he was looking for somebody coming with a bike there. He look around. He look around me. I act like I was dying. I said to the other guy, hold me, act like I cannot walk, you know? He looked at me and then first looked at the other guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then with the loudspeaker, the guy with the bike, come on the double. I hear nothing. I don't know nothing. So we they got there, they can't find us. So we ate the food, and it gave me some more strength. I don't eat food  like that for ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from there, we keep going and going. Then after three or four days, we step close to another river. Everybody was thirsty like hell. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the water was but 100 feet away from us, and we're not allowed to go there to drink water. Anybody goes close, they just shot on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people got crazy, couldn't take it anymore. You know, the thirst makes you crazy, you know, the fever and everything. They shoot quite a few people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was somebody I knew from Greece. He was an engineer. And he says to us, with the crew people, he says, listen, guys, he says, to my estimation, I think we are so close to the river. If you guys dig something, you're going to find some water close by here. We don't have to go drink from the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was right. I always used to carry a big spoon with me. So we start digging with the spoon, and before you know, by that deep, here's water coming down. It was a thing of water in the sand and drink it-- whatever, worms, whatever was there, everything tastes good. And there gave us also a little moisture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before we-- make the long story short, before we arrive in Germany, there was a train waiting for us, because they don't want the German public to see they was taking so many thousands of people on the road like that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of them, they were dead-- 3/4 dead, top. Anybody fall down is shot and in the truck, shot and in the truck. You have to keep going with the pace, or they threw the dog on top of you. Because each German used to have a German Shepherd with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrive in Germany, after we crossed, I don't know, Poland, Ukraine, I don't know what countries we crossed walking, after 17 or 18 days walking. And from there, they took us to Dachau on the train. How many people started on the walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 23,000, I hear. We arrived, 1,700. The rest of them died on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrive in Warsaw. It was heaven. They had toilets, showers, you know? Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Showers, toilets, you know? And I saw a lot of people, officers, over there from different countries, so war prisoners, with stars here, with uniform. Which place is that? I don't even know what's Dachau. After a while, I find out, you know, it was Dachau. So over there, by the kitchen, was carrots and potatoes and everything. I used to sneak in, take a carrot, a potato. I start eating them from there. It was not as bad as Auschwitz and Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we stay there for a few days, probably a week or two, they took us from there in another place. It was 11 camps, Camp 1 till 11, different camps, about two or three miles apart from each other, concentration camps, working camps, We had to clear out forests.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one was near Landsberg. The city was near Landsberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, I think that's the name of the city, the closest city that was to the camps. It was all forests around. And they put us to chop trees down. That way, they can make a road toward-- the tractors and all of those things can go through there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked there for a while. It was not as bad as Birkenau, but there was not very much food. When the people start dying, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they make a thing-- a [GERMAN]. It means if people were to die, they have to bury them somewhere. So they picked me up with a few other people. We had two big trucks, two big wagons with wheels. And we used to put them around the shoulder, and we used to carry them. And in each one, we used to put about 100 dead people. From all 11 camps that had died, I used to bury them, with the other people, not all by myself. And we used to put 200, 300 like sardines-- head, foot, foot, head, like that, you know? Like the head here, the foot here, then turn around, you know, pile all that up. We used to dig a hole maybe about 10 feet deep, and maybe almost as big as this room here. And we used to put about 200 people dead there, pile up one on top of the other one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then after we work there for, I would say, about 8, 9 months. Right around 1945-- it was February, I think it was, because I remember the snow-- so all of a sudden, they took us from there. They said they closed the camp, and they was going to take us somewhere. They don't say where.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just keep marching, day in and day out, day in and day out. And we  didn't know where we were going. Then finally, we hear they're going to take us in a deserted place, and they're going to shoot every one of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend if mine, he spoke-- his name was Daniel Hazan. I got a picture from him. He hear that from two Germans talking together, because he spoke German. I didn't at the time. Now I speak good German, but at that time, I didn't. So they took us, keep going and going, day in and day out, day in and day out, no food, hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, let me see, sometime end of April-- I would say sometime about April the 25th, 24th-- we arrive in a forest. It was very much digging.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that guy, Daniel Hazan, which I have a picture from him, and another 8 people together, we agree. I say, we've got nothing to lose. They're going to shoot us anyway. Let's take a run to the forest. Whatever it is, is going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said, OK, we agree. So we, certain time. As soon as we go in the middle of the forest, then the other edge, you know? We're going to get on the road, because we're going in the middle of the road. And we're going to take a run. So we got there. We took a run. And that way, the Germans opened the machine guns. Two of us dropped dead. So we keep running in the forest. They chased us for a while. They surely thought probably they're going to kill us, all of them. We keep running and running and running. I started to have cuts here, here, here, all over, from the branch from the trees, running in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we got exhausted, we fall inside in a big hole. And I don't know how long I was there unconscious. It was one day, two days, three days, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the hole, one on top of  the other one, falling.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ground, in a hole, yeah. Was a big hole. We don't know how long it was there. So in the hole, we find some potato peels inside the hole. I don't know if it was for catch wild pigs, or something. I don't know what it was. So we find potato peels there, so we ate the potato peels. They were frozen. We ate them. After we were there-- like I said before, I don't know if it was one day or two days. I passed out. I don't know. I don't remember. So we don't have no watch, no time. We don't know nothing. The only time we know-- day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we figure, what are we going to do now? We hear, bombs, boom, shooting everything, bombs and weapons, but we don't know what's happening. We have no idea. So we say, let's go out to the forest and, slowly, let's go take a look at what's happening. So we keep walking and walking. All of a sudden, at night, we saw-- because we're afraid to go in the daytime. Because then somebody is going to see us. And we had this crazy uniform with the stripes. They can spot you right away. You're a prisoner from the camp. We didn't have civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, far away, we saw a little light. We went close carefully. We went and we saw a light, and we were watching over there. All of a sudden, we saw somebody with one leg. He had a bucket, something in the hand. And he had a-- like the cowboys done, they used to give the horses to drink. He dumped a bucket there. We don't know what it-- then all of a sudden, the pigs start coming and grabbing the food, something there to eat from this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he come back for another bucket, a few buckets. And the pigs  was making so much noise, so we figure, what are we going to do? We had a hat around, like a big yarmulke. It goes around like that. So we sneak in slowly, slowly. We then push the pigs. We put the thing with the yarmulke full of lice. We pick up some food, and we run back to the forest. We ate that. Do you remember what it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate that, and the German guy, he come, he look around. He couldn't see nobody, but we saw him, you know? We wait till he goes inside the house, because, you know, one of those wasn't enough. I can eat the whole thing from the pigs by myself. [LAUGHS] So he went, he would go inside over there. We pick another one, we run out. The second time, we come down and run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the third time, we went there. We pick up. Probably, he was watching from the door, or something. He comes with a shotgun. He saw us, you know, running with the food from the pigs, because the pigs were so noisy, like inviting come out somebody, you know? And he starts shooting at us. So luckily he don't hit anybody. So we were afraid to go back over there. So we were so cold at night, freezing. But 200, 300 yards from the thing, we saw a barrack. It was a barn. And we saw something smoke-- damp come out from the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went inside, and we opened the door, the window [INAUDIBLE]. And it was, I think, full of fertilizer, but warm. We took a dive in the fertilizer. We covered up to the head. That's from the cow, but it was warm. Oh, god, like a Jacuzzi. And then we covered up. We stayed there all night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our bones got warm from the filth. Nobody can smell nothing at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right before dawn, we hear somebody talking from the outside, two of them. And it sounds like two cats was fighting. And I don't know what it was. I say to my friend, let's see, I don't know who they are. It doesn't sound German. Because it doesn't sound German. It doesn't sound Russian-- because I was with a lot of Russian prisoners in the camp and French. And I don't know what kind of language is it. I never hear that before. So I said to my friend, let's get prepared. We look around, we find a two by four. Let's get ready, and see if somebody's going to attack us. Whoever it is, we're going to hit him, you know? Because we don't want to go back. If we go back to the camp is-- for sure, they're going  to kill us. So, all of a sudden, we saw the point of a machine gun go over the little window over there. And then we saw a helmet. And then he says, hands up, he says, in English. But I don't spoke English at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to my friend, in Spanish, I said, [SPANISH]. It means in Spanish-- because we speak jargon. The Greek Jews speak jargon in Spanish. They call it Ladino.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy turned around, and he says, hablas espanol? It turned out to be it was an American-Mexican soldier. He look at us, and he says in Spanish, but where you guys come from? We told  him we were prisoners from Germany. He says, they put the Spaniards there, the Germans? He got so mad, cursing, and everything. So he says, what do you want me to do for you? He was  here with another guy, with another Mexican. I told him, now we're just waiting for the Americans and the Russians to come. We're the Americans. You're the Americans? Yeah. So then he said, you want a cigarette? You want something? The guy don't know what to do with us, the Mexican-American soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after a little while, he gave us a little-- he didn't have much food with him-- a little rations. We took anything he had-- a little can, a little thing. We ate everything he had. And he asked for his buddy too to give it to us. But it was nothing for us. I could eat the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we told him what the guy did over there, further down. He got so mad. He says to me, come, you guys, on the jeep. He says, we're going to go take care of this god-damned Nazi. So he had a separate gun in there. He handed me a gun, the machine gun. And he handed another gun to another guy. He had only two guns and a machine gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went over there. We showed  him where the place was. We went over there. He hit one with the gun in front. He hit, he looked. Then the guy was shaking all over, the German in one leg. Then we spoke German, but I didn't. But I could speak Spanish to him. I'd been an interpreter. And I told him in German, I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you shot on us, now you're going to pay for it. He said, please, in German, I don't like Hitler myself. Look at what happened to me. He says, I lost a leg in the war. I lost a son in the war. He says, and one other son, I don't know where  he is, somewhere in Russia. He starts crying, and everything, please, don't kill me, blah, blah, blah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I feel sorry for the guy, believe it or not. Because he showed he had a broken leg. He showed the picture from his son with the Iron Cross, where he died. So we figure, probably, he's not as guilty as we thought. He was protecting his property, or something. Then he says to me and to the other friends, the Mexican soldier, he says, you go here and take anything you want. I said to the guy, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Where you keep your food? He says, in the keller. And I was pointing the gun at him. So we went down. We took a barrel of marmalade up to here from there. We took as much we could carry with us, food  from there. And we went away because we tried to follow the soldier, because we were afraid of the Nazis. He says, I'm in the front over there. He says, but if you go a few miles back, you're going to see the rest of the soldiers coming. So we tried to go in the direction he was going. And the points are out, because if you go that way, you see the German is over there. This side is the American army. So we took the food. We went back to the fertilizer place, and we sit over there, because it was nice and warm. Then we start eating and eating. And we start to eat early in the morning. It was already night, ans we still even don't stop eating. That's how hungry we was. So the next day, everybody probably was so sick. I had diarrhea, vomiting, and everything. And two of my friends died for overeating. So we went back. We meet the army. They died right then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died a few days later. They drained themselves with diarrhea and couldn't stop it. Before he gets up, he has to go back again. The system couldn't take so much food. We ate non-stop. My stomach was up to here, but still, I was hungry inside my mind. The two, they don't together. Eat and eat, and we're still hungry. It doesn't matter. I couldn't get full.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went, and we start walking back where the soldiers was coming. And then we figure that already was occupied by the American soldiers. And we stayed in a village over there. After a few days, we start coming to ourselves. Then the war was over already. It was May the 5th, somewhere around there, or a few days later. But the war was over. Germany gave up. So by that time, we got in strength too. So we start talking between us. What are we going to do to pay those guys back something? So when we went to the German guy over there, I saw he had a wire-cutter. We went looking for food. We went back over there. The guy left me the pistol, the soldier. He says, take it for protection.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back over there, and I said, I want the wire-cutter what you got. So take it, take it, take it. So we took the wire-cutter. And then there was thousands of jeeps from the soldiers over there, some laughing, joking, blah, blah, blah. So what we did is we decided we're going to start burning those places over there With the wire-cutters-- the American soldiers had five gallons of gasoline in the back for reserve. We got the wire-cutter. We stole the gasoline, and each one take a gasoline. And we had matches from the soldiers that gave us before. Because he gave us [INAUDIBLE] matches. So we start pouring around the villages over there. But we don't burn the guy's house. We feel sorry for him, the one with the one leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], it means the city of forest, [NON-ENGLISH]. So we start burning anything we can burn, and then we run away. After a few days later, you know, everything calmed down-- in the beginning, for a week or two, we'd get away with everything we wanted to do. Then the mayor from there called up the American soldiers. He says, somebody is burning the cities. And they sent soldiers over there. And then when they catch us doing this, the American soldiers start shooting  at us. And one of our guys got killed from them, the American soldiers. We were burning the city. So then we see it's end of it. We cannot do this no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the soldiers, they said, you guys have to go toward the end, where the prisoners are after the war. They said, you guys cannot be-- what you call that-- anarchists, what you call it? You know, they call them rebels. You cannot live that kind, because you guys are going to get arrested. They're going to put you in jail. You cannot do that. You have to go back where the rest of the camps are, the other refugees are. So they told us if you walk, there's a place called Feldafing. And Feldafing, it was a nice place there. We had a good time. We started living our normal life over there. There we had steady food, and everything. That was after the war, now. Yeah, after the war finished, and the Germans lost the war. So we had good food. And then after three or four months, they say, who wants to go to Palestine? Anybody under 20. I was at the time, about 19 years old, or 18. So anybody under 20 can go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went. I got a picture too from there that we went into. We were in a truck. They took us to Salzburg, Austria. From Salzburg, Austria, they was going to sneak us to go to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there a few months. I don't know if I should come back to my cousin again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then by accident, we were in a place where we had to get [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means food stamps to eat. Anybody don't have a place to stay from the war, in certain places, like here, you go there in the office, and they gave you food stamps to buy food with that, with no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. Everybody was there. So me, I gave them about 10 purses-- my brother, my sister. They gave me a bunch of food stamps, and I can get  more food. You know, they all were dead, but  I gave  it anyway, and they believed me. Then, all of a sudden, I look at it, and saw my cousin Albert there. I couldn't believe it. How the guy got there in Salzburg? And he was a very good-looking fellow, a very handsome fellow. He was about 8, 9 years older than me. So because he spoke very good German, the secretary from the-- she was writing the names and everything else, he tried to make it up with the lady, to go on a date with her. So I look on it, and I act like he doesn't exist.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he beat me up, I was about 16 years old, so three years passed. And I was weak at the time. After three years, I got three years older, and I got my strength back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to another guy, to Daniel Hazan, the one I just mentioned to you, the one who spoke also German-- he also spoke German good. Because his father used to have a store in Greece, a hardware store. And he used to deal a lot with the Germans. And that's why he picked up the German language. A big hardware store. It's very large. So he said-- the girl wants to go with him, you know, and he doesn't want to carry the food stamps and wait. He said to Daniel Hazan, he says to him, why don't you take the food stamps and give it to my cousin Albert. He will hold it for me. I Turn around to him, and I say, are you crazy? I'm not  your cousin. He say, what? say, yeah, I'm not your cousin. You remember what you did to me? He said to me, you want some more? You don't got enough over there? You want some more? I said, I want to see how good you are now. I tell him, I'm not a Muselmann. Muselmann means those living skeletons, they used to call them in Birkenau and Auschwitz. Somebody was skin and bones, they called them Muselmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: And second, I say, Albert, I'm not 16 years old anymore. I'm 19 now. And I said, you just try something, you're going to get the surprise of your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we have to stop there.
